Notes - Current Assets: 1. Cash & Bank Balance is taken directly from the line ‘Cash and cash equivalents’ in the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position. 2. ‘Investments’ aggregates the ‘Other investments’ line under Current Assets; derivative financial instruments are treated as ‘Other Current Assets’ to avoid double‑counting, as they are not explicitly classified as short‑term investments in the source. 3. ‘Inventory’ maps to the ‘Inventories’ line. 4. ‘Receivables’ is the sum of ‘Trade and other receivables’ and ‘Income tax receivable’. 5. ‘Prepaid Expenses’ and ‘Loans & Advances’ are not disclosed in the current‑assets section and are therefore reported as zero. 6. ‘Other Current Assets’ includes only the ‘Derivative financial instruments’ line; ‘Assets held for sale’ is excluded because it is a separate classification and not part of the CMA current‑asset categories. 7. Total Current Assets are taken from the ‘Total current assets’ line in the source statement; the aggregated sub‑components sum exactly to this figure, confirming no double‑counting. 8. All numeric values are presented in plain digits (no commas) and retain the original sign and scale. 9. No note references were attached to the extracted amounts; any footnote references were omitted from the numeric values as per the instructions.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: No non‑current asset line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Current Liabilities: No current liability line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: No non‑current liability line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Equity: No equity line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: $m
$m
$m

Notes - Current Assets: All current‑asset line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: 1. **Aggregation Logic** – Each CMA attribute was populated by summing the relevant line items from the Non‑Current Assets section of the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  The mapping follows the RBI/IBA CMA nomenclature:
• *Fixed Assets (Net Block)* = Property, Plant & Equipment + Right‑of‑Use Assets.
• *Investments (Non‑Current)* = Investments in Associates & Joint Ventures + Other Investments + Derivative Financial Instruments.
• *Intangible Assets* = Intangible Assets + Goodwill (Goodwill is treated as an intangible asset under the CMA framework).
• *Deferred Tax Assets* = Deferred Tax Assets.
• *Other Non‑Current Assets* = Other Receivables.
• *Capital Work‑in‑Progress* and *Loans & Advances* were not disclosed in the source and are therefore set to zero.

2. **Year‑wise Discrepancies** – For 2024 and 2023 the sum of the mapped categories (76,192 and 76,192 respectively) exceeds the reported Total Non‑Current Assets (76,065) by 127 and 127 units.  This suggests that one or more line items may be double‑counted or that the source total excludes a small component (e.g., a minor re‑classification).  The 2022 figures reconcile exactly (73,890).  The discrepancy is flagged for manual review.
3. **Mapping Assumptions** –
• Goodwill is included in Intangible Assets as per RBI/IBA CMA guidance.
• Derivative Financial Instruments are treated as part of Investments (Non‑Current).
• Right‑of‑Use Assets are combined with Property, Plant & Equipment to form Fixed Assets (Net Block).
4. **Ambiguities Flagged** –
• The source does not explicitly state whether Right‑of‑Use Assets are current or non‑current; they are treated as non‑current for consistency with the CMA format.
• The slight mismatch in 2024/2023 totals indicates a potential re‑classification that is not captured in the table; this requires confirmation from the auditor’s notes.

5. **Notes to the Auditor** – The auditor should verify the classification of Right‑of‑Use Assets and confirm whether any component of the Total Non‑Current Assets has been omitted or re‑classified in the source.

Notes - Current Liabilities: All current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: All non‑current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Equity: All equity line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Current Assets: All values are in $m. 2025 is not reported in the balance sheet.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: All values are in $m.

Notes - Current Liabilities: All values are in $m and are negative to reflect liability balances. 2025 is not reported in the balance sheet. 2024, 2023 and 2022 are presented in the order requested.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: None

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: Long‑Term Borrowings = Interest‑bearing loans & borrowings + Lease liabilities; Provisions (Non‑Current) = Provisions + Retirement benefit obligations; Other Non‑Current Liabilities = Derivative financial instruments + Income tax payable + Other payables. All figures are taken from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position and are presented as negative values to reflect liability balances. No ambiguities were flagged.

Notes - Current Assets: 1. Cash & Bank Balance is taken directly from the line ‘Cash and cash equivalents’ in the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position. 2. ‘Investments’ aggregates the ‘Other investments’ line under Current Assets; derivative financial instruments are treated as ‘Other Current Assets’ to avoid double‑counting, as they are not explicitly classified as short‑term investments in the source. 3. ‘Inventory’ maps to the ‘Inventories’ line. 4. ‘Receivables’ is the sum of ‘Trade and other receivables’ and ‘Income tax receivable’. 5. ‘Prepaid Expenses’ and ‘Loans & Advances’ are not disclosed in the current‑assets section and are therefore reported as zero. 6. ‘Other Current Assets’ includes only the ‘Derivative financial instruments’ line; ‘Assets held for sale’ is excluded because it is a separate classification and not part of the CMA current‑asset categories. 7. Total Current Assets are taken from the ‘Total current assets’ line in the source statement; the aggregated sub‑components sum exactly to this figure, confirming no double‑counting. 8. All numeric values are presented in plain digits (no commas) and retain the original sign and scale. 9. You are extracting Current Assets data from a Balance Sheet page and converting it into the RBI/IBA‑approved CMA format.

*** Task ***
- Extract all line items under “Current Assets” from the provided content.
- Extract values for all years presented (e.g., 2025, 2024).
  1. Process each year independently.
  2. Do not mix or sum values across years.
- Map and aggregate the extracted data into the CMA structure below, strictly in this order:
  1. Cash & Bank Balance
  2. Investments
  3. Inventory
  4. Receivables
  5. Prepaid Expenses
  6. Loans & Advances
  7. Other Current Assets
  8. Total Current Assets

*** Rules ***
- Recognize alternate labels (e.g., Cash and Cash Equivalents, Short‑term Investments, Inventories, Trade and Other Receivables, Prepayments, Contract Assets).
- If multiple source line items map to one CMA attribute, aggregate them separately for each year.
- Exclude subtotals when detailed components are summed to avoid double counting.
- Capture note references (e.g., “Note 12”) as text metadata only, never as amounts.
- Ensure ‘Total Current Assets’ is captured separately and not confused with ‘Total Assets’.
- Use main table values unless notes provide a more granular breakdown; document this in Notes.
- Normalize all numeric values to plain digits only (no commas or separators); preserve negatives and unit/scale.
- If a line item is ambiguous, flag it for manual review instead of double mapping.

*** Output Requirements ***
- Always output all CMA Current Asset attributes for each year, even if some values are missing.
- Use RBI/IBA CMA terminology for headers.
- Include a “Notes – Current Assets” section (≤300 words) documenting - Year‑wise aggregation logic, Mapping assumptions, Any ambiguities flagged

The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit

risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised

fair value not attributable to market risk.

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit
risk. Under IFRS 9 ‘Financial Instruments’, the Group records the component of fair value changes relating to the component of own credit
risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in
fair value not attributable to market risk.
The interest rates used to discount future cash flows for fair value adjustments, where applicable, are based on market swap curves at the
reporting date, and were as follows:

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.
Notes to the Group Financial Statements AstraZeneca Annual Report & Form 20-F Information 2024 181

All derivatives are held at fair value and fall within Level 2 of the fair value hierarchy as defined in Note 12, except for an equity warrant which
falls within Level 3 (valued at $nil (2023: $12m; 2022: $19m), held within Non-current assets). None of the derivatives have been reclassified
in the year. The equity warrant expired on 31 December 2024. Its value at that date was recorded as zero.
The fair value of interest rate swaps and cross-currency swaps is estimated using appropriate zero coupon curve valuation techniques to
discount future contractual cash flows based on rates at the current year end.
The fair value of forward foreign exchange contracts and currency options are estimated by cash flow accounting models using appropriate
yield curves based on market forward foreign exchange rates at the year end. The majority of forward foreign exchange contracts for existing
transactions had maturities of less than one month from year end.

Strategic Report Corporate Governance Financial Statements Additional Information
The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit
risk. Under IFRS 9 ‘Financial Instruments’, the Group records the component of fair value changes relating to the component of own credit
risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in
fair value not attributable to market risk.
The interest rates used to discount future cash flows for fair value adjustments, where applicable, are based on market swap curves at the
reporting date, and were as follows:
2024 2023 2022
Loans and borrowings 2.0% to 2.9% n/a to n/a1 4.3% to 4.9%
1 All bonds designated as FVPL in 2023 matured prior to the reporting date.
20 Trade and other payables
2024 2023 2022
$m $m $m
Current liabilities
Trade payables 3,640 3,267 2,550

Non-current liabilities
Accruals 65 36 37
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities – 7 14
Contingent consideration 581 1,171 1,465
Acerta Pharma share purchase liability – – 779
Other payables 1,124 1,446 1,975
Total 1,770 2,660 4,270
Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.
Notes to the Group Financial Statements AstraZeneca Annual Report & Form 20-F Information 2024 181

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

You are a financial analyst with expertise in corporate reporting, your function is to extract key attributes from Financial Reports including Balance Sheets and Income Statements. 
Current task is to extract the Currency, Denomination and Financial Years from the Balance Sheet.
The Balance Sheet may be in any format (US GAAP, IFRS, CMA, etc.).

*** Locate the Currency and Dates Section ***
Identify the "Consolidated Balance Sheet" header.
Within it, just above the "Assets" section you will find the Currency, Denomination and the Financial Years. 

*** References ***
Currency could be represented in ISO 4217 standards or in full e.g. INR, CHF, USD, Indian Rupees, Swiss Francs, US Dollar
Financial Dates could be represented just as the year or may include date, month and year.
Current Year will always be 1 year greater than the previous year.
Denomination may be "in Millions" or "in Pounds"

*** Instructions ***
Always output the currency in ISO 4217 standard
If only the Year is provided show the Financial Years as a 4 digit number.
Extract and Display the dates in "DD-MON-YYYY" if both Date and Month is present, else Extract and Display the dates in "YYYY" format.

where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.

Notes - Current Assets: All current‑asset line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: 1. **Aggregation Logic** – Each CMA attribute was populated by summing the relevant line items from the Non‑Current Assets section of the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  The mapping follows the RBI/IBA CMA nomenclature:
• *Fixed Assets (Net Block)* = Property, Plant & Equipment + Right‑of‑Use Assets.
• *Investments (Non‑Current)* = Investments in Associates & Joint Ventures + Other Investments + Derivative Financial Instruments.
• *Intangible Assets* = Intangible Assets + Goodwill (Goodwill is treated as an intangible asset under the CMA framework).
• *Deferred Tax Assets* = Deferred Tax Assets.
• *Other Non‑Current Assets* = Other Receivables.
• *Capital Work‑in‑Progress* and *Loans & Advances* were not disclosed in the source and are therefore set to zero.

2. **Year‑wise Discrepancies** – For 2024 and 2023 the sum of the mapped categories (76,192 and 76,192 respectively) exceeds the reported Total Non‑Current Assets (76,065) by 127 and 127 units.  This suggests that one or more line items may be double‑counted or that the source total excludes a small component (e.g., a minor re‑classification).  The 2022 figures reconcile exactly (73,890).  The discrepancy is flagged for manual review.
3. **Mapping Assumptions** –
• Goodwill is included in Intangible Assets as per RBI/IBA CMA guidance.
• Derivative Financial Instruments are treated as part of Investments (Non‑Current).
• Right‑of‑Use Assets are combined with Property, Plant & Equipment to form Fixed Assets (Net Block).
4. **Ambiguities Flagged** –
• The source does not explicitly state whether Right‑of‑Use Assets are current or non‑current; they are treated as non‑current for consistency with the CMA format.
• The slight mismatch in 2024/2023 totals indicates a potential re‑classification that is not captured in the table; this requires confirmation from the auditor’s notes.

5. **Notes to the Auditor** – The auditor should verify the classification of Right‑of‑Use Assets and confirm whether any component of the Total Non‑Current Assets has been omitted or re‑classified in the source.

Notes - Current Liabilities: All current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: All non‑current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Equity: All equity line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

nodes_executed.resolved_input:
You are extracting Non‑Current Assets data from a Balance Sheet page and converting it into the RBI/IBA‑approved CMA format.

*** Task ***
- Extract all line items under “Non‑Current Assets” from the provided content.
- Extract values for all years presented (e.g., 2025, 2024).
  1. Process each year independently.
  2. Do not mix or sum values across years.
Map and aggregate the extracted data into the CMA structure below, strictly in this order:
  1. Fixed Assets (Net Block)
  2. Capital Work‑in‑Progress
  3. Investments (Non‑Current)
  4. Intangible Assets
  5. Deferred Tax Assets
  6. Loans & Advances
  7. Other Non‑Current Assets
  8. Total Non‑Current Assets
  9. Total Assets

*** Rules ***
- Recognize alternate labels (e.g., Property, Plant & Equipment, Goodwill, Financial Assets, Investments in Associates/JVs, Deferred Tax Asset).
- If multiple source line items map to one CMA attribute, aggregate them separately for each year.
- Exclude subtotals when detailed components are summed to avoid double counting.
- Capture note references (e.g., “Note 10”) as text metadata only, never as amounts.
- Ensure ‘Total Non‑Current Assets’ is captured separately and not confused with ‘Total Assets’.
- Use main table values unless notes provide a more granular breakdown; document this in Notes.
- Normalize all numeric values to plain digits only (no commas or separators); preserve negatives and unit/scale.
- If a line item is ambiguous, flag it for manual review instead of double mapping.

*** Output Requirements ***
- Always output all CMA Non‑Current Asset attributes for each year, even if some values are missing.
- Use RBI/IBA CMA terminology for headers.
- Include a “Notes – Non‑Current Assets” section (≤300 words) documenting - Year‑wise aggregation logic, Mapping assumptions, Any ambiguities flagged

The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit

risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in
fair value not attributable to market risk.

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in

where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.

collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;

nodes_executed.resolved_input:
You are extracting Current Liabilities data from a Balance Sheet page and converting it into the RBI/IBA‑approved CMA format.

*** Task ***
- Extract all line items under “Current Liabilities” from the provided content.
- Extract values for all years presented (e.g., 2025, 2024).
  1. Treat each year independently.
  2. Do not mix or sum values across different years.
- Map and aggregate the extracted data into the CMA structure below, strictly in this order:
  1. Short‑Term Borrowings
  2. Trade Payables
  3. Provisions
  4. Current Maturities of Long‑Term Debt
  5. Current Tax Liabilities
  6. Other Current Liabilities
  7. Total Current Liabilities

*** Rules ***
- Recognize alternate labels (e.g., Trade and Other Payables, Financial Debt, Accruals, Current Portion of Long‑Term Debt, Income Tax Payable, Derivative Liabilities).
- If multiple source line items map to one CMA attribute, aggregate them separately for each year.
- Exclude subtotals when detailed components are summed to avoid double counting.
- Capture note references (e.g., “Note 14”) as text metadata only, never as amounts.
- Ensure ‘Total Current Liabilities’ is captured separately and never confused with ‘Total Liabilities’.
- Normalize all numeric values to plain digits only (no commas or separators); preserve negatives and unit/scale.
- If a line item is ambiguous, flag it for manual review instead of double mapping.

*** Output Requirements ***
- Always output all CMA Current Liabilities attributes for each year, even if some values are missing.
- Use RBI/IBA CMA terminology for headers.
- Include a “Notes – Current Liabilities” section (≤300 words) documenting - Year‑wise aggregation logic, Mapping assumptions, Any ambiguities flagged

Strategic Report Corporate Governance Financial Statements Additional Information
The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit
risk. Under IFRS 9 ‘Financial Instruments’, the Group records the component of fair value changes relating to the component of own credit
risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in
fair value not attributable to market risk.
The interest rates used to discount future cash flows for fair value adjustments, where applicable, are based on market swap curves at the
reporting date, and were as follows:

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in

Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.

collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;

nodes_executed.resolved_input:
You are extracting Non‑Current Liabilities data from a Balance Sheet page and converting it into the RBI/IBA‑approved CMA format.

*** Task ***
- Extract all line items under “Non-Current Liabilities” from the provided content.
- Extract values for all years presented (e.g., 2025, 2024).
  1. Treat each year independently.
  2. Do not mix or sum values across different years.
- Map and aggregate the extracted data into the CMA structure below, strictly in this order:
  1. Long‑Term Borrowings
  2. Deferred Tax Liabilities
  3. Provisions (Non‑Current)
  4. Other Non‑Current Liabilities
  5. Total Non‑Current Liabilities
  6. Total Liabilities

*** Rules ***
- Recognize alternate labels (e.g., Financial Debt, Deferred Tax, Employee Benefit Obligations, Derivative Liabilities, Other Payables).
- If multiple source line items map to one CMA attribute, aggregate them separately for each year.
- Exclude subtotals when detailed components are summed to avoid double counting.
- Capture note references (e.g., “Note 18”) as text metadata only, never as amounts.
- Ensure ‘Total Non‑Current Liabilities’ is captured separately and not confused with ‘Total Liabilities’.
- Use main table values unless notes provide a more granular breakdown; document this in Notes.
- Normalize all numeric values to plain digits only (no commas or separators); preserve negatives and unit/scale.
- If a line item is ambiguous, flag it for manual review instead of double mapping.

*** Output Requirements ***
- Always output all CMA Non‑Current Liabilities attributes for each year, even if some values are missing.
- Use RBI/IBA CMA terminology for headers.
- Include a “Notes – Non‑Current Liabilities” section (≤300 words) documenting - Year‑wise aggregation logic, Mapping assumptions, Any ambiguities flagged

Strategic Report Corporate Governance Financial Statements Additional Information
The cumulative adjustment to the carrying value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge relationship in the year was an increase in the
liability of $16m. A loss of $2m was made during the year on the fair value of bonds designated in a fair value hedge, due to increased credit
risk. Under IFRS 9 ‘Financial Instruments’, the Group records the component of fair value changes relating to the component of own credit
risk through Other comprehensive income. Changes in credit risk had no material effect on any other financial assets and liabilities recognised
at fair value in the Group Financial Statements. The change in fair value attributable to changes in credit risk is calculated as the change in
fair value not attributable to market risk.
The interest rates used to discount future cash flows for fair value adjustments, where applicable, are based on market swap curves at the
reporting date, and were as follows:

Included within Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are contract liabilities of $114m (2023: $102m; 2022: $87m).
The revenue recognised in the year from opening contract liabilities is $96m, comprising $89m relating to other revenue accruals and $7m
Collaboration Revenue contract liabilities. The major markets with Rebates, chargebacks, returns and other revenue accruals are the US
where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $4,978m (2023: $5,116m; 2022: $3,961m), of which Rare Disease comprises $240m
(2023: $190m; 2022: $139m), and China where the liability at 31 December 2024 amounted to $532m (2023: $567m; 2022: $579m).
Trade payables includes $105m (2023: $123m; 2022: $67m) due to suppliers that have signed up to a supply chain financing programme,
under which the suppliers can elect on an invoice-by-invoice basis to receive a discounted early payment from the relationship bank rather
than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to
the relationship bank rather than the supplier. The value of the liability payable by the Group remains unchanged. The Group assesses the
arrangement against indicators to assess if debts which vendors have sold to the funder under the supplier financing scheme continue to
meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

meet the definition of trade payables or should be classified as borrowings. At 31 December 2024, the payables met the criteria of Trade
payables. The supply chain financing programme operates in the US, UK, Sweden, China and Germany, and as at 31 December 2024, the
programme had 458 suppliers enrolled across these countries.
Vaccine contract liabilities relate to amounts received from customers, primarily government bodies, in advance of supply of product.
Included within current Other payables are liabilities to Daiichi Sankyo totalling $377m (2023: $199m; 2022: $100m) resulting from the
collaboration agreement in relation to Enhertu entered into in March 2019. Additionally, included within non-current Other payables are
liabilities totalling $456m (2023: $774m; 2022: $1,125m) as a result of the Enhertu collaboration agreement and $462m (2023: $464m;
2022: $nil) owed to Avillion as a result of the Airsupra collaboration agreement entered into in March 2018.

than being paid in line with the agreed payment terms. If the option is taken, the Group’s liability is assigned by the supplier to be due to

nodes_executed.resolved_input:
You are extracting Equity data from a Balance Sheet page and converting it into the RBI/IBA‑approved CMA format.

*** Task ***
- Extract all line items under “Equity” from the provided content.
- Identify all year columns present in the Equity table dynamically (e.g., 2025, 2024, 2023, or more) using column headers, captions, or table structure.
- Extract Equity values for every identified year, without exception..
  1. Process each year independently.
  2. Do not mix or sum values across years.
- Map and aggregate the extracted data into the CMA structure below, strictly in this order:
  1. Share Capital
  2. Reserves & Surplus
  3. Treasury Shares
  4. Non‑Controlling Interests
  5. Other Equity Components
  6. Total Equity
  7. Total Liabilities and Equity

*** Rules ***
- Recognize alternate labels (e.g., Equity Share Capital, Retained Earnings, Other Reserves, Translation Reserve, Treasury Stock, Minority Interest).
- If multiple source line items map to one CMA attribute, aggregate them separately for each year.
- Exclude subtotals when detailed components are summed to avoid double counting.
- Capture note references (e.g., “Note 15”) as text metadata only, never as amounts.
- Ensure ‘Total Equity’ is captured separately and not confused with ‘Total Liabilities and Equity’.
- Use main table values unless notes provide a more granular breakdown; document this in Notes.
- Normalize all numeric values to plain digits only (no commas or separators); preserve negatives and unit/scale.
- If a line item is ambiguous, flag it for manual review instead of double mapping.

*** Output Requirements ***
- Always output all CMA Equity attributes for each year, even if some values are missing.
- Use RBI/IBA CMA terminology for headers.
- Include a “Notes – Equity” section (≤300 words) documenting - Year‑wise aggregation logic, Mapping assumptions, Any ambiguities flagged, Explicit confirmation that all available years in the source were extracted

*** Content Details ***
Content :

Notes - Current Assets: 1. Cash & Bank Balance is taken directly from the line ‘Cash and cash equivalents’ in the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position. 2. ‘Investments’ aggregates the ‘Other investments’ line under Current Assets; derivative financial instruments are treated as ‘Other Current Assets’ to avoid double‑counting, as they are not explicitly classified as short‑term investments in the source. 3. ‘Inventory’ maps to the ‘Inventories’ line. 4. ‘Receivables’ is the sum of ‘Trade and other receivables’ and ‘Income tax receivable’. 5. ‘Prepaid Expenses’ and ‘Loans & Advances’ are not disclosed in the current‑assets section and are therefore reported as zero. 6. ‘Other Current Assets’ includes only the ‘Derivative financial instruments’ line; ‘Assets held for sale’ is excluded because it is a separate classification and not part of the CMA current‑asset categories. 7. Total Current Assets are taken from the ‘Total current assets’ line in the source statement; the aggregated sub‑components sum exactly to this figure, confirming no double‑counting. 8. All numeric values are presented in plain digits (no commas) and retain the original sign and scale. 9. No note references were attached to the extracted amounts; any footnote references were omitted from the numeric values as per the instructions.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: No non‑current asset line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Current Liabilities: No current liability line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: No non‑current liability line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Equity: No equity line items were extracted for the purpose of this exercise.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: $m
$m
$m

Notes - Current Assets: All current‑asset line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: 1. **Aggregation Logic** – Each CMA attribute was populated by summing the relevant line items from the Non‑Current Assets section of the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  The mapping follows the RBI/IBA CMA nomenclature:
• *Fixed Assets (Net Block)* = Property, Plant & Equipment + Right‑of‑Use Assets.
• *Investments (Non‑Current)* = Investments in Associates & Joint Ventures + Other Investments + Derivative Financial Instruments.
• *Intangible Assets* = Intangible Assets + Goodwill (Goodwill is treated as an intangible asset under the CMA framework).
• *Deferred Tax Assets* = Deferred Tax Assets.
• *Other Non‑Current Assets* = Other Receivables.
• *Capital Work‑in‑Progress* and *Loans & Advances* were not disclosed in the source and are therefore set to zero.

2. **Year‑wise Discrepancies** – For 2024 and 2023 the sum of the mapped categories (76,192 and 76,192 respectively) exceeds the reported Total Non‑Current Assets (76,065) by 127 and 127 units.  This suggests that one or more line items may be double‑counted or that the source total excludes a small component (e.g., a minor re‑classification).  The 2022 figures reconcile exactly (73,890).  The discrepancy is flagged for manual review.
3. **Mapping Assumptions** –
• Goodwill is included in Intangible Assets as per RBI/IBA CMA guidance.
• Derivative Financial Instruments are treated as part of Investments (Non‑Current).
• Right‑of‑Use Assets are combined with Property, Plant & Equipment to form Fixed Assets (Net Block).
4. **Ambiguities Flagged** –
• The source does not explicitly state whether Right‑of‑Use Assets are current or non‑current; they are treated as non‑current for consistency with the CMA format.
• The slight mismatch in 2024/2023 totals indicates a potential re‑classification that is not captured in the table; this requires confirmation from the auditor’s notes.

5. **Notes to the Auditor** – The auditor should verify the classification of Right‑of‑Use Assets and confirm whether any component of the Total Non‑Current Assets has been omitted or re‑classified in the source.

Notes - Current Liabilities: All current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: All non‑current‑liability line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Equity: All equity line items are taken directly from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position.  No additional granularity was required for the RBI/IBA CMA mapping.

Notes - Current Assets: All values are in $m. 2025 is not reported in the balance sheet.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: All values are in $m.

Notes - Current Liabilities: All values are in $m and are negative to reflect liability balances. 2025 is not reported in the balance sheet. 2024, 2023 and 2022 are presented in the order requested.

Notes - Non-Current Assets: None

Notes - Non-Current Liabilities: Long‑Term Borrowings = Interest‑bearing loans & borrowings + Lease liabilities; Provisions (Non‑Current) = Provisions + Retirement benefit obligations; Other Non‑Current Liabilities = Derivative financial instruments + Income tax payable + Other payables. All figures are taken from the Consolidated Statement of Financial Position and are presented as negative values to reflect liability balances. No ambiguities were flagged.
